title: Model Checking Matrix Product States against Linear Chain Logic
authors: Ming Xu, Yihao Chen, Ji Guan
abstract: Matrix product states (MPS) are a standard tensor-network representation for ground states of one-dimensional quantum many-body systems, and they underpin widely used simulation tools such as DMRG. However, while quantum model checking has been developed mainly for quantum programs and communication protocols (with properties expressed along a time axis), there is still no comparable framework for systematically verifying \emph{spatial} and \emph{size-dependent} properties of physical many-body states, where the key parameter is the system size. This paper takes a step toward bridging the gap. We propose \emph{Linear Chain Logic} (LCL), a spatial logic designed to specify physically meaningful properties of periodic MPS families as the system size grows, such as nontriviality on rings and large-size asymptotic patterns. Our approach builds on a simple but powerful connection: every periodic MPS naturally induces a completely positive map (a quantum operation) on its virtual space, so many quantitative features of the MPS can be analysed through the repeated application of the operation. Using this perspective, we derive an effective procedure to compute the inner products of an MPS at a given size and to support richer LCL specifications, without relying on brute-force state expansion. We then develop approximate model-checking algorithms that combine sound bounding with asymptotic structural analysis, enabling scalable reasoning about large system sizes. Experiments on representative MPS families illustrate that our method can automatically verify nontriviality and detect asymptotic spatial regimes in a way that complements traditional numerical techniques.
summary: This paper proposes a new spatial logic called Linear Chain Logic (LCL) to formally verify properties of 1D quantum many-body states as a function of system size. By leveraging the transfer-operator structure of Matrix Product States, the authors develop an efficient algorithm to detect asymptotic patterns like correlation decay and phase transitions. This work bridges the gap between quantum model checking and the numerical analysis of tensor networks.
main_problem: The lack of a formal framework for verifying spatial and size-dependent properties of 1D many-body states (Matrix Product States) as the system size grows.
main_result: The introduction of Linear Chain Logic (LCL) and an approximate model-checking algorithm that can automatically verify properties like state validity, correlation decay, and phase transitions in MPS families.
method: A transfer-operator-based approach that reduces MPS properties to the spectral analysis of a completely positive map, using semilinear over/under-approximations to compute evidence sets for LCL formulas.
model_or_system: One-dimensional periodic Matrix Product States (MPS) representing ground states of translationally invariant 1D local Hamiltonians, such as the TFIM, XXZ, and Kitaev models.
key_observables: Squared norms, correlation functions, energy differences, decay ratios, and indicators for phase transitions or symmetry breaking.
important_parameters: System size (N), bond dimension (D), spectral radius, and peripheral eigenvalues.
main_assumptions: The logic is specifically designed for periodic MPS families; the algorithm relies on approximations due to the undecidability of the Skolem problem; the 'Until' operator is excluded from the logic syntax.
figures_summary: Tables showing the decision results (True/False/Unknown/Timeout) and runtimes for various synthetic and physical MPS models across different bond dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the verification gap, proposes the LCL syntax, establishes the mathematical connection between MPS and CP maps, develops the top-down and bottom-up model-checking algorithms, and validates the method using synthetic and physical spin-chain models.
why_interesting: It provides a formal, logic-based verification layer for tensor network simulations, allowing researchers to systematically certify the asymptotic behavior and stability of many-body states as they approach the thermodynamic limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Schematic overview of a 1D quantum many-body system and its MPS.
